title: Liquid level sensing apparatus and related methods
abstract: Liquid level sensing apparatus are disclosed. An example liquid level sensing apparatus includes a cannula defining a body having a tip and an access channel. The tip to pierce a container, where the cannula is to be at least partially positioned in the container when the tip pierces the container. A probe is to be positioned in the access channel. A signal source is to electrically energize the probe and the cannula to cause the probe to emit a first signal and cause the cannula to emit a second signal.
claims:
Claims What Is Claimed Is: 1. A liquid level sensing apparatus comprising: a cannula defining a body having a tip and an access channel, the tip to pierce a container, the cannula to be at least partially positioned in the container when the tip pierces the container; a probe to be positioned in the access channel; and a signal source to electrically energize the probe and the cannula to cause the probe to emit a first signal and cause the cannula to emit a second signal. 2. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the first signal is electrically phase controlled relative to the second signal. 3. The apparatus of claim 2, wherein the first and second signals are controlled via phase-lock loop control system. 4. The apparatus of claim 1 , further including an antenna adj acent the container to detect a transmission of the first signal when the probe contacts a liquid in the container and the probe is positioned through the access channel of the cannula. 5. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the cannula and the probe are composed of an electrically conductive material. 6. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the first signal and the second signal are radio frequency signals. 7. The apparatus of claim 1, wherein the second signal emitted by the cannula is to prevent degradation of the first signal emitted by the probe when the probe is positioned in the access channel of the cannula. 8. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the cannula does not interfere with the first signal emitted by the probe. 9. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein neither the probe nor the cannula are electrically grounded. 10. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the first signal is electrically in phase with the second signal. 1 1. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein the first signal is electrically phase shifted from the second signal. 12. The apparatus of claim 1 , wherein both the probe and the cannula are electrically grounded. 13. The apparatus of claim 1, wherein at least one of the first signal or the second signal includes a waveform having a sine wave, a square wave, a triangular wave, or a sawtooth wave. 14. A liquid level sensing apparatus comprising: a cannula composed of a conductive material, the cannula to pierce a seal of a container, the cannula being movable relative to the container in a first direction and a second direction, the first direction being different than the second direction, the cannula including an opening passing through a first end of the cannula and a second end of the cannula to define a passageway, the cannula to emit a first signal when at least a portion of the cannula is positioned in a cavity of the container; a probe composed of a conductive material, the probe being movable relative to the cannula in the first direction and the second direction, the probe to emit a second signal, the probe to pass through the passageway provided by the cannula to aspirate a sample from the container; and a signal generator operatively coupled to the cannula and the probe, the signal generator to provide the first signal to the cannula and the second signal to the probe. 15. The apparatus of claim 14, wherein the first signal is electrically in phase with the second signal. 16. The apparatus of claim 14, wherein the first signal is electrically phase shifted from the second signal. 17. The apparatus of claim 14, wherein neither the probe nor the cannula are electrically grounded. 18. The apparatus of claim 14, wherein both the probe and the cannula are electrically grounded. 19. The apparatus of claim 14, further including an antenna to receive the second signal of the probe when the probe is inside the container and the passageway of the cannula. 20. The apparatus of claim 14, wherein the cannula is to emit the first signal while the probe is moving through the passageway of the cannula. 21. The apparatus of claim 19, wherein the first signal emitted by the cannula is to prevent degradation of the second signal emitted by the probe when the probe is positioned in the passageway of the cannula. 22. A method for sensing liquid in a container, the method comprising: providing a first signal to a probe to cause the probe to emit a first electrical field; providing a second signal to a cannula to cause the cannula to emit a second electrical field; moving the cannula to pierce a seal of a container to provide access to a liquid in a cavity of the container; and moving the probe through a passageway of the cannula and into the cavity of the container while the probe emits the first electrical field and the cannula emits the second electrical field. 23. The method of claim 22, further including positioning an antenna adjacent a bottom surface of the container, the antenna to receive the first signal emitted by the probe. 24. The method of claim 22, further including detecting a change in the first signal and determining if the detected change in the first signal is greater than a threshold. 25. The method of claim 24, further including stopping movement of the probe when the detected change in the first signal is greater than the threshold. 26. The method of claim 25, further including aspirating a portion of the liquid in the cavity of the container when the probe is positioned through the passageway of the cannula. 27. The method of claim 22, further including electrically grounding neither the probe nor the cannula. 28. The method of claim 22, further including electrically grounding both the probe and the cannula. 29. A non-transitory computer-readable medium comprising instructions that, when executed, cause a machine to: provide a first signal to a probe to cause the probe to emit a first electrical field; provide a second signal to a cannula to cause the cannula to emit a second electrical field; move the cannula to pierce a seal of a container to provide access to a liquid in a cavity of the container; and 

 move the probe through a passageway of the cannula and into the cavity of the container while the probe emits the first electrical field and the cannula emits the second electrical field. 30. The non-transitory machine readable storage of claim 29, wherein when executed, further cause the machine to receive the first signal emitted by the probe via an antenna positioned adjacent a bottom surface of the container. 31. The non-transitory machine readable storage of claim 29, wherein when executed, further cause the machine to detect a change in the first signal and determine if the detected change in the first signal is greater than a threshold. 32. The non-transitory machine readable storage of claim 31 , wherein when executed, further cause the machine to stop movement of the probe when the detected change in the first signal is greater than the threshold. 33. The non-transitory machine readable storage of claim 29, wherein when executed, further cause the machine to aspirate a portion of the liquid in the cavity of the container when the probe is positioned through the passageway of the cannula.
